insight: Strategic synergy between units should take priority over evaluating individual unit strength. Players should think in terms of 'I can go for units 1, 2, and 5 together' rather than 'unit 1 is the strongest unit, how do I use it.' The exception is absorb units (e.g., Centurion), which are strong enough that their individual power becomes the primary consideration.
author: crash_overlord
context: Discussion about unit efficiency spreadsheets and tier lists prompted crash_overlord to articulate his strategic framework.

insight: Rhino is considered a weak unit overall: in its best-case scenario (absorbing on the prompt turn, getting two clicks, soaking for 2, opponent bought Steelsplitter), it is roughly comparable to or slightly better than Tarsier, but still below genuinely strong units like Perforator. Its average and worst-case performance is poor, placing it in the bottom 25% of all units.
author: consensus
context: Extended community debate about Rhino's power level, with awaclus, crash_overlord, silentslayers, and shadourow all contributing assessments.

insight: Rhino is most valuable in a mono-red build where it serves as a defender (absorber), while Steelsplitter is weak as an attacker. If forced to choose between them, Rhino has more situational defensive value in mono-red, but if you can buy either, Steelsplitter is generally the better purchase.
author: shadourow
context: Discussion comparing Rhino and Steelsplitter utility in mono-color builds.

insight: Buying a Steelsplitter that simply gets Wall'd is roughly equivalent in value to buying two Drones, but the comparison depends on what you can follow it up with: if you can push over the absorb barrier soon, Steelsplitter is better; if not, Drones are better. In the vast majority of cases (estimated ~90%), buying Drones is the correct choice over Steelsplitter when the Splitter will just be absorbed.
author: consensus
context: awaclus and silentslayers debated the value of buying a Steelsplitter versus Drones when the Splitter would be absorbed.

insight: Spending Blue on Steelsplitter is so inefficient that it can be worse than wasting the Blue entirely. Buying a Pixie with leftover Blue is generally better than buying a Steelsplitter with it, because spending Blue on Splitter is sometimes worse than doing nothing.
author: awaclus
context: awaclus elaborated on why spending Blue on Steelsplitter is especially poor, in comparison to other suboptimal uses of Blue.

insight: Centurion is considered one of the best units in the game and is a primary example of an absorb unit whose individual power makes it nearly unskippable in sets where it appears.
author: consensus
context: Multiple experts (silentslayers, shadourow, crash_overlord) agreed on Centurion's top-tier status during unit evaluation discussion.

insight: Pixie is only good to buy when your Blue would otherwise be wasted (effectively making it free at 1 cost). Buying a Blastforge primarily to generate Pixies is a poor strategy; the comparison to buying a Tarsier with that investment illustrates why. Each additional Blue spent on Pixies when better options exist is individually bad for the same reason.
author: awaclus
context: awaclus explained Pixie's correct use case in response to a new player overestimating its power.

insight: Pixie creates a persistent threat that forces the opponent to overdefend once (e.g., buy a Husk). As long as you don't click the Pixie, the opponent's defensive answer remains tied up addressing it. You should click Pixie only when it exploits a lack of granularity in the opponent's defenses or kills a vigilant unit.
author: awaclus
context: awaclus explained the strategic nuance of when to activate Pixie versus holding it as a threat.

insight: A small number of attackers is worse than buying no attackers at all, because the gold spent on them is diverted from economy and they get cancelled by a single Wall. Attackers become efficient en masse; the better the available defense in the random set, the longer you can delay buying attackers and reinvest in economy for a more overwhelming late attack.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy explained the absorb/attack economy tradeoff to a new player asking why defense wins in Prismata.

insight: Blood Rager is strong specifically as a vehicle to reinvest early attack (which would otherwise be absorbed) into something useful. It is poor when bought in any other role. This category of unit—converting early wasted attack into value—is distinct and context-dependent.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy explained Bloodrager's correct role in response to a new player's assessment of it as middle-of-the-pack.

insight: Blood Pact gives a Grimbotch with 3 lifespan instead of the standard version; the extra turn of delay makes it significantly worse. The tradeoff is that Blood Pact can be a necessary defensive option even when bad for you, if the alternative is not defending at all.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy and awaclus discussed Blood Pact's mechanics and situational use.

insight: Units with expensive tech requirements or high cost are partially balanced by the opponent's ability to punish with a fast attack before those units come online. This is a key reason why raw efficiency calculations for expensive units overstate their practical value.
author: megasupp
context: megasupp noted this as an important caveat when discussing unit efficiency spreadsheets.

insight: Venge Cannon is considered a very high-impact unit that appears in a massive proportion of games, making it one of the most important random set units despite community tier lists sometimes placing it lower.
author: silentslayers
context: silentslayers criticized a tier list that placed Venge Cannon too low, stating that a massive proportion of games are decided by it.

insight: Colossus is extremely powerful when it can absorb 7 damage the turn after being bought, but is considered 'clunky' because achieving that timing in practice is difficult.
author: shadourow
context: shadourow commented on Colossus's power ceiling versus its practical clunkiness.

insight: Selling drones via units like Ebb Turbine is generally good because by the late game, buying drones is bad. A practical heuristic for Ebb Turbine: click Ebb at the start of your turn, then if you decide you actually want a drone, unclick Ebb instead of buying the drone. This reframes the hard decision of 'should I sell a drone?' into the easier decision of 'should I buy a drone?'
author: amalloy
context: amalloy shared a decision-making heuristic for Ebb Turbine to help players know when to sell drones.

insight: In many situations it is correct to neither buy nor sell a drone—specifically when you are already generating exactly enough gold to optimally spend all your tech. Selling a drone just to float gold is a significant mistake.
author: awaclus
context: awaclus clarified that drone-selling decisions must account for the case where no drone transaction is correct.

insight: Master Bot (MB) tends to buy drones too late into the game, well past when drone purchases are economically efficient. An Ebb Turbine strategy exploits this indirectly by removing that bad choice from the bot's decision tree.
author: velizar_
context: velizar_ observed that the bot keeps buying drones when it shouldn't, and noted that Ebb Turbine clicks remove that poor option.

insight: Master Bot struggles to defend against Scorchilla rush strategies. masn6811 tested a Scorchilla-based rush against the bot, won, and re-tested several months later to find it still worked, suggesting the bot's defense against this line had not improved.
author: masn6811
context: masn6811 recalled repeatedly beating MB with a Scorchilla rush over multiple sessions months apart.

insight: Aegis is considered weak enough by community experts that it was flagged as needing a buff. Its primary weakness is that Tarsiers have a slight efficiency edge over it, and if the opponent can attack for 6, Aegis becomes very poor as an absorber. Additionally, playing red when Aegis is in the set creates a perverse incentive to get a Blastforge over a Conduit, worsening your tech synergy.
author: masn6811
context: masn6811 analyzed Aegis's weaknesses in depth after a new player initially thought it was horrible, then revised upward.

insight: Plexo Cell is considered the best absorber in the game by at least one expert ranking, followed by Polywall and then Aegis. Plexo is praised for combining high efficiency with ease of purchase, and masn6811 states it is better than every other absorber except in sets with Chill or Offensive Finesse.
author: consensus
context: silentslayers ranked absorbers as Plexo > Polywall > Aegis; masn6811 endorsed Plexo's superiority with noted exceptions.

insight: Amporilla is rarely part of the Nash equilibrium in most sets—neither player optimally buys it—but its presence warps the set because it is a massive threat if the opponent allows you to build toward it freely.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy assessed Amporilla's set-warping threat value versus its low direct playrate.

insight: 'Gauss Cannon' is sometimes jokingly called 'Goose' by community members as a deliberate mispronunciation for fun rather than a genuine regional dialect difference.
author: amalloy
context: amalloy clarified the origin of the 'Goose' nickname for Gauss Cannon.
